title: Data Insights: Multi-Source Reasoning
prerequisites: read a short text or table for a specific fact
combine two given facts to reach a conclusion
learning_objectives: Identify which source holds each fact a question needs.
Combine facts across two or three sources to reach the answer.
Ignore sources that do not bear on the question.

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do -> we do -> you do

opening.builds_on: Reading a text or table for a fact and combining two facts into a conclusion.

do_now.instructions: No notes. Answer from memory in 4 minutes.

items.prompt: Text A: 'the store opens at 9 a.m.' Text B: 'deliveries arrive 2 hours after opening.' When do deliveries arrive?
A price list shows $15 and a coupon gives 10% off. What is the paid price?
items.targets: applying a rule from one source to a value in another

items.prompt: A question asks only about cost. Must you use a source that lists only dates?
items.answer: No - use only the sources carrying the facts you need.

retrieval_starter.purpose: Recalls cross-referencing two facts and using only the sources a question needs - the core moves of Multi-Source Reasoning.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: Have them underline the one fact each source contributes before answering.

questions.prompt: In a multi-source problem, where does the answer usually come from?
questions.answer: Combining facts across two or more sources, not one alone.

questions.prompt: Do you have to use every source to answer a question?
questions.answer: No - only the ones holding the facts the question needs.

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: You can pull a fact from a text or table and combine two facts into a conclusion. Multi-Source Reasoning gives you two or three short sources (like tabs) and asks a question whose answer usually lives in the COMBINATION of them. Your job is to find which source holds each needed fact, ignore the rest, and put the pieces together.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can locate the relevant facts across two or three sources and combine them to answer a question, ignoring any source that does not bear on it.
success_criteria: I identify exactly what the question is asking for.
I find which source holds each fact I need.
I combine those facts (and skip irrelevant sources) to reach the answer.

opening_script.move: Do Now on screen; learners answer from memory, no notes.
Cold-call answers; name the argument's conclusion vs premises.
Bridge to today's question type; state what the stem is really asking.
Move to I do: model the reasoning on one worked item.

i_do.stem:
Source 1 - Notice: The Riverside Community Center offers Pottery, Painting, and Weaving classes. Each class session is listed at $15.
Source 2 - Email: Members pay 20% less than the listed price for every session.

Question: A member attends one Pottery session and one Painting session. What total does she pay?
i_do.explanation: The question needs the price (Source 1) and the member rule (Source 2). Listed price is $15 per session; members pay 20% less, so $12 per session. Two sessions at the member price: 2 x $12 = $24, option A. $30 ignores the discount, and $27 discounts only one session.
think_aloud_steps: The question asks the total a member pays for two sessions - I need the price and the member rule.
Source 1 gives the listed price: $15 per session.
Source 2 gives the member rule: 20% less, so $15 - $3 = $12 per session.
Two sessions at the member price: 2 x $12 = $24.
That is option A; $30 ignores the discount and $27 discounts only one session.
i_do.key_takeaway: In Multi-Source Reasoning the answer usually comes from combining sources: find which source holds each needed fact, apply any rule, and integrate - do not answer from a single source when another one modifies it.

we_do.stem:
Source 1 - Schedule: Shuttle A leaves the North Lot at 9:00 a.m.; Shuttle B leaves the South Lot at 9:20 a.m. Each shuttle takes 20 minutes to reach campus.
Source 2 - Notice: The North Lot is closed all week for construction.
Source 3 - Email: Dr. Lee must be on campus by 9:45 a.m.

Question: Which shuttle can Dr. Lee take and still arrive on time?

options.A: Shuttle A from the North Lot.
options.B: Shuttle B from the South Lot.
options.D: Shuttle A from the South Lot.

we_do.explanation: Source 2 closes the North Lot, so Shuttle A is out. Shuttle B leaves the South Lot at 9:20 and takes 20 minutes, arriving 9:40 - before the 9:45 deadline in Source 3. So the answer is Shuttle B from the South Lot, option B.
scaffold_hints: First, which lots are usable? Check the closure notice.
The North Lot is closed, so any shuttle from it is out.
For the remaining shuttle, add its 20-minute ride to its departure and compare with the 9:45 deadline.

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Right - the North Lot is closed, and Shuttle B (9:20 + 20 min = 9:40) beats the 9:45 deadline.
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Combine all three sources: the closure rules out the North Lot shuttle, then check the other shuttle's arrival against 9:45.

we_do.stem:
Source 1 - Room rates: Standard $90 per night; Deluxe $130 per night; Suite $180 per night.
Source 2 - Policy: Any stay of 3 or more nights receives $20 off per night on any room.

Question: A guest books a Deluxe room for 3 nights. What is the total room cost?
we_do.explanation: Source 1 lists Deluxe at $130 per night. Source 2 gives $20 off per night for stays of 3 or more nights, so $110 per night. Three nights: 3 x $110 = $330, option C. $390 forgets the discount; $370 applies it to only one night.
scaffold_hints: Find the Deluxe nightly rate in the price table.
Check the discount policy - does a 3-night stay qualify?
Apply the per-night discount, then multiply by the number of nights.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: The 3-night stay earns the discount on EVERY night; apply it to all three, then total.

you_do.stem:
Source 1 - Email: Our book club has 5 members, and we want one copy of the new novel for each member.
Source 2 - Store listing: The novel costs $18 in hardcover and $11 in paperback.
Source 3 - Note: The treasury has $70, and everyone must receive the same format.

Question: Which format can the club afford for all five members?

options.E: Hardcover for three members and paperback for two.

you_do.explanation: Hardcover for all five: 5 x $18 = $90, which is more than the $70 budget - unaffordable. Paperback for all five: 5 x $11 = $55, within budget - affordable. Source 3 requires the same format for everyone, so the split option is out. The answer is paperback only, option D.

you_do.stem:
Source 1 - Timetable: Train X arrives at Central Station at 2:10 p.m.
Source 2 - Bus notice: The museum bus leaves Central Station every 30 minutes starting at 2:00 p.m. (2:00, 2:30, 3:00, and so on); the ride takes 25 minutes.
Source 3 - Email: I need to reach the museum by 3:00 p.m.

Question: If the traveler takes Train X, what is the earliest she can arrive at the museum?
you_do.explanation: Train X arrives at 2:10, so the first catchable bus is the 2:30 (the 2:00 has already left). 2:30 plus the 25-minute ride is 2:55 p.m., which meets the 3:00 deadline. The answer is 2:55 p.m., option E. 2:35 wrongly assumes a bus leaves the moment she arrives.

you_do.stem:
Source 1 - Inventory: Warehouse A holds 120 units; Warehouse B holds 90 units of the product.
Source 2 - Order: A customer orders 150 units, to ship from a single warehouse if possible, otherwise split between warehouses.
Source 3 - Policy: A split shipment (two warehouses) adds a $25 fee; single-warehouse shipping is free.

Question: Will the order incur the split-shipment fee, and why?

options.A: Yes; neither warehouse alone holds 150 units, so the order must be split.
options.B: No; Warehouse A alone can fill the order.
options.C: No; Warehouse B alone can fill the order.
options.D: No; the order can be split with no fee.
options.E: Yes; the customer asked to split the shipment to save money.

you_do.explanation: The order is 150 units. Warehouse A holds 120 and Warehouse B holds 90, so neither can fill 150 alone; the order must ship from both, which triggers the $25 split-shipment fee (Source 3). The answer is A.

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.
